title: Motion Sickness
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
I hate you for what you did
  > [#1, 109 votes] The first of several internal contradictory statements (here rhymed) that expresses the singer’s confusion as to why she could hate someone and miss someone at the same time after a breakup. If the...
And I miss you like a little kid
I faked it every time, but that's alright
  > [#2, 122 votes] This line is a double entendre. The first part of the entendre states that throughout the relationship the narrator did not really have feelings for the man. She says it’s alright because she can...
I can hardly feel anything
I hardly feel anything at all
You gave me fifteen hundred
  > [#3, 89 votes] Hypnotherapy is a form of alternative medicine in which a professional puts a patient into a trance-like state and makes suggestions that can alter patient’s bad behaviors. It is most often used to...
To see your hypnotherapist
I only went one time, you let it slide
Fell on hard times a year ago
Was hopin' you would let it go and you did

[Chorus]
I have emotional motion sickness
  > [#4, 110 votes] Ryan Adams, who is the real life person the song is about, had Ménière’s disease at the time. This is a condition whose symptoms are similar to those of motion sickness, including nausea, vomiting...
Somebody roll the windows down
  > [#5, 61 votes] Motion sickness is common in cars. Windows on older cars were “rolled down” with a handle that you spun. Opening the window is felt to alleviate the symptoms of motion sickness and accompanying...
There are no words in the English language
  > [#6, 75 votes] Ryan Adam’s behavior follows her everywhere she goes, and it haunts her. It’s also common of abusers to gaslight their victims, so that the victim of abuse will get angry, then feel like they’re the...
I could scream to drown you out

[Verse 2]
I'm on the outside looking through
  > [#7, 86 votes] This line references the idiom “Don’t throw stones in glass houses.” Now that she’s separated from her partner, she can see them clearly, as if through a pane of glass, and is able to recognize that...
You're throwing rocks around your room
And while you're bleeding on your back in the glass
I'll be glad that I made it out
And sorry that it all went down like it did

[Chorus]
I have emotional motion sickness
  > [#4, see above]
Somebody roll the windows down
  > [#5, see above]
There are no words in the English language
  > [#6, see above]
I could scream to drown you out
Hey, why do you sing with an English accent?
  > [#8, 89 votes] From a 2003 NME review of a concert by Ryan Adams in Dublin: Some fans however were left bemused by the New Yorker’s rather bizarre behaviour as he performed some songs in an English accent.
I guess it's too late to change it now
  > [#9, 15 votes] Often, when people spend a lot of time together, particular features of another get on one’s nerves, even something as innocuous as an accent. What Bridgers may be referring to here, is the idea that...
You know I'm never gonna let you have it
But I will try to drown you out
  > [#10, 57 votes] This contrasts the earlier chorus, which says: There are no words in the English language I could scream to drown you out This indicates growth and attempting to at least try to move past the pain,...

[Bridge]
You said when you met me, you were bored
  > [#11, 122 votes] The fact that the person she met was in a band when she was born suggests that he is much older than the singer. His motivation for pursuing the singer’s affects seems to be some kind of mid-life...
You said when you met me, you were bored
And you, you were in a band when I was born

[Chorus]
I have emotional motion sickness
  > [#4, see above]
I try to stay clean and live without
  > [#12, 65 votes] In the final chorus, Bridgers invokes language used when people talk about addiction, saying she hopes to metaphorically “stay clean and live without” her ex and their toxic relationship. But the use...
And I wanna know what would happen
If I surrender to the sound
Surrender to the sound
